title: Who made us?
body: God made us.

title: Who is God?
body: God is the Supreme Being, infinitely perfect, who made all things and keeps them in existence.

title: Why did God make us?
body: God made us to show forth His goodness and to share with us His everlasting happiness in heaven.

title: What must we do to gain the happiness of heaven?
body: To gain the happiness of heaven we must know, love, and serve God in this world. Lay not up to yourselves treasures on earth; where the rust and moth consume and where thieves break through and steal.

title: From whom do we learn to know, love, and serve God?
body: We learn to know, love, and serve God from Jesus Christ, the Son of God, who teaches us through the Catholic Church.

title: Where do we find the chief truths taught by Jesus Christ through the Catholic Church?
body: We find the chief truths taught by Jesus Christ through the Catholic Church in the Apostles' Creed.

title: Say the Apostles' Creed.
body: I believe in God, the Father Almighty, Creator of heaven and earth; and in Jesus Christ, His only Son, Our Lord; who was conceived by the Holy Ghost, born of the Virgin Mary, suffered under Pontius Pilate, was crucified, died and was buried. He descended into hell; the third day He arose again from the dead; He ascended into heaven, sitteth at the right hand of God, the Father Almighty; from thence He shall come to judge the living and the dead. I believe in the Holy Ghost, the Holy Catholic Church, the communion of Saints, the forgiveness of sins, the resurrection of the body, and life everlasting. Amen.

title: What do we mean when we say that God is the Supreme Being?
body: When we say that God is the Supreme Being we mean that He is above all creatures, the self-existing and infinitely perfect Spirit.

title: What is a spirit?
body: A spirit is a being that has understanding and free will, but no body, and will never die. To whom then have you likened God?

title: What do we mean when we say that God is self-existing?
body: When we say that God is self-existing we mean that He does not owe His existence to any other being.

title: What do we mean when we say that God is infinitely perfect?
body: When we say that God is infinitely perfect we mean that He has all perfections without limit.

title: What are some of the perfections of God?
body: Some of the perfections of God are: God is eternal, all-good, all-knowing, all-present, and almighty.

title: What do we mean when we say that God is eternal?
body: When we say that God is eternal we mean that He always was and always will be, and always remains the same. "

title: What do we mean when we say that God is all-good?
body: When we say that God is all-good we mean that He is infinitely lovable in Himself, and that from His fatherly love every good comes to us. For the word of the Lord is right; and all his works are done with faithfulness.

title: What do we mean when we say that God is all-knowing?
body: When we say that God is all-knowing we mean that He knows all things, past, present, and future, even our most secret thoughts, words, and actions. Behold, O Lord, thou hast known all things, the last and those of old: thou hast formed me, and hast laid thy hand upon me.

title: What do we mean when that God Is all-present?
body: When we say that God is all-present we mean that He is everywhere.

title: If God is everywhere, why do we not see Him?
body: Although God is everywhere, we do not see Him because He is a spirit and cannot be seen with our eyes.

title: Does God see us?
body: God sees us and watches over us with loving care. Be not solicitous therefore, saying: "What shall we eat?" or "What shall we drink?" or "Wherewith will we be clothed?" For after all these things do the heathens seek.

title: What is God's loving care for us called?
body: God's loving care for us is called Divine Providence.

title: What do we mean when we say that God is almighty?
body: When we say that God is almighty we mean that He can do all things.

title: Is God all-wise, all-holy, all-merciful, and all-just?
body: Yes, God is all-wise, all-holy, all-merciful, and all-just.

title: Can we know by our natural reason that there is a God?
body: We can know by our natural reason that there is a God, for natural reason tells us that the world we see about us could have been made only by a self-existing Being, all-wise and almighty. For the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and injustice of those men that detain the truth of God in injustice; because that which is known of God is manifest in them.

title: Can we know God in any other way than by our natural reason?
body: Besides knowing God by our natural reason, we can also know Him from supernatural revelation --that is, from the truths, found in Sacred Scripture and in Tradition, which God Himself has revealed to us.

title: What do we mean when we say that God has revealed these truths to us?
body: When we say that God has revealed these truths to us we mean that He has made them known to certain persons, to be announced to their fellow men as the word of God.

title: What is the Bible?
body: The Bible is the written word of God, committed to His Church for the instruction and sanctification of mankind.

title: What do we mean when we say that the entire Bible is inspired?
body: When we say that the entire Bible is inspired we mean that its principal author is God, though it was written by men whom God enlightened and moved to write all those things, and only those things, that He wished to be written.

title: How is the Bible divided?
body: The Bible is divided into the Old Testament, written before the coming of Jesus Christ, and the New Testament, written after His ascension into heaven.

title: Are all the passages of the Bible to be understood according to our modern manner of expression?
body: No; some of the passages of the Bible are not to be understood according to our modern manner of expression, since they contain certain figures of speech, parables, and literary forms used by the people of ancient times but not employed in the present.

title: How can we know the true meaning of the Bible?
body: We can know the true meaning of the Bible from the teaching authority of the Catholic Church, which has received from Jesus Christ the right and the duty to teach and to explain all that God has revealed.

title: Are Catholics encouraged by the Church to read the Bible?
body: Yes; Catholics are encouraged by the Church to read the Bible, especially the Gospels, which tell about the earthly life of Jesus Christ, the Son of God made man.

title: What is the chief message of the New Testament?
body: The chief message of the New Testament is the joyful announcement of our salvation through Jesus Christ.

title: What is Divine Tradition?
body: Divine Tradition is the unwritten word of God – that is, truths revealed by God, though not written in the Bible, and given to the Church through word of mouth by Jesus Christ or by the apostles under the inspiration of the Holy Ghost.

title: Has Divine Tradition ever been committed to writing?
body: Divine Tradition has been committed to writing, especially by saintly writers called Fathers, who lived in the early centuries but were not inspired, as were those who wrote the Bible.

title: Has Divine Tradition the same force as the Bible?
body: Yes; Divine Tradition has the same force as the Bible, since it too contains God's revelation to men.

title: By what kind of act do we believe the doctrines contained in the Bible and in Divine Tradition?
body: We believe the doctrines contained in the Bible and Divine Tradition by an act of divine faith, which means that we accept them on the authority of God, who can neither deceive nor be deceived.

title: Is there only one God?
body: Yes, there is only one God.

title: How many Persons are there in God?
body: In God there are three Divine Persons – the Father, the Son, and the Holy Ghost.

title: Is the Father God?
body: The Father is God and the first Person of the Blessed Trinity.

title: Is the Son God?
body: The Son is God and the second Person of the Blessed Trinity.

title: Is the Holy Ghost God?
body: The Holy Ghost is God and the third Person of the Blessed Trinity.

title: What do we mean by the Blessed Trinity?
body: By the Blessed Trinity we mean, one and the same God in three Divine Persons.

title: Are the three Divine Persons really distinct from one another?
body: The three Divine Persons are really distinct from one another. And the Holy Ghost descended in a bodily shape, as a dove, upon him. And a voice came from heaven: "Thou art my beloved Son.

title: Are the three Divine Persons perfectly equal to one another?
body: The three Divine Persons are perfectly equal to one another, because all are one and the same God.

title: How are the three Divine Persons, though really distinct from one another, one and the same God?
body: The three Divine Persons, though really distinct from one another, are one and the same God because all have one and the same Divine nature.

title: Can we fully understand how the three Divine Persons, though really distinct from one another, are one and the same God?
body: We cannot fully understand how the three Divine Persons, though really distinct from one another, are one and the same God because this is a supernatural mystery.

title: What is a supernatural mystery?
body: A supernatural mystery is a truth which we cannot fully understand, but which we firmly believe because we have God's word for it.

title: What do we mean when we say that God is the Creator of heaven and earth?
body: When we say that God is the Creator of heaven and earth we mean that He made all things from nothing by His almighty power.

title: Which are the chief creatures of God?
body: The chief creatures of God are angels and men.

title: What are angels?
body: Angels are created spirits, without bodies, having understanding and free will.

title: What gifts did God bestow on the angels when He created them?
body: When God created the angels He bestowed on them great wisdom, power, and holiness.

title: Did all the angels remain faithful to God?
body: Not all the angels remained faithful to God; some of them sinned. And there was a battle in heaven; Michael and his angels battled with the dragon, and the dragon fought and his angels.

title: What happened to the angels who remained faithful to God?
body: The angels who remained faithful to God entered into the eternal happiness of heaven, and these are called good angels.

title: What do the good angels do in heaven?
body: In heaven the good angels see, love, and adore God.

title: How do the good angels help us?
body: The good angels help us by praying for us, by acting as messengers from God to us, and by serving as our guardian angels. For to his angels he has given thee in trust, to keep thee in all thy ways.

title: How do our guardian angels help us?
body: Our guardian angels help us by praying for us, by protecting us from harm, and by inspiring us to do good.

title: What happened to the angels who did not remain faithful to God?
body: The angels who did not remain faithful to God were cast into hell, and these are called bad angels, or devils.

title: What is the chief way in which the bad angels try to harm us?
body: The chief way in which the bad angels try to harm us is by tempting us to sin.

title: Do all temptations come from the bad angels?
body: Some temptations come from the bad angels; but other temptations come from ourselves and from the persons and things about us.

title: What is man?
body: Man is a creature composed of body and soul, and made to the image and likeness of God.

title: Is this likeness to God in the body or in the soul?
body: This likeness to God is chiefly in the soul.

title: How is the soul like God?
body: The soul is like God because it is a spirit having understanding and free will, and is destined to live forever.

title: Who were the first man and woman?
body: The first man and woman were Adam and Eve, the first parents of the whole human race.

title: What was the chief gift bestowed on Adam and Eve by God?
body: The chief gift bestowed on Adam and Eve by God was sanctifying grace, which made them children of God and gave them the right to heaven.

title: What other gifts were bestowed on Adam and Eve by God?
body: The other gifts bestowed on Adam and Eve by God were happiness in the Garden of Paradise, great knowledge, control of the passions by reason, and freedom from suffering and death.

title: What commandment did God give Adam and Eve?
body: God gave Adam and Eve the commandment not to eat of the fruit of a certain tree that grew in the Garden of Paradise. And he commanded him, saying: "Of every tree of paradise thou shalt eat; but of the tree of knowledge of good and evil, thou shalt not eat.

title: Did Adam and Eve obey the commandment of God?
body: Adam and Eve did not obey the commandment of God, but ate of the forbidden fruit.

title: What happened to Adam and Eve on account of their sin?
body: On account of their sin Adam and Eve lost sanctifying grace, the right to heaven, and their special gifts; they became subject to death, to suffering, and to a strong inclination to evil, and they were driven from the Garden of Paradise.

title: What has happened to us on account of the sin of Adam?
body: On account of the sin of Adam, we, his descendants, come into the world deprived of sanctifying grace and inherit his punishment, as we would have inherited his gifts had he been obedient to God.

title: What is this sin in us called?
body: This sin in us is called original.

title: Why is this sin called original?
body: This sin is called original because it comes down to us through our origin, or descent, from Adam.

title: What are the chief punishments of Adam which we inherit through original sin?
body: The chief punishments of Adam which we inherit through original sin are: death, suffering, ignorance, and a strong inclination to sin.

title: Is God unjust in punishing us on account of the sin of Adam?
body: God is not unjust in punishing us on account of the sin of Adam, because original sin does not take away from us anything to which we have a strict right as human beings, but only the free gifts which God in His goodness would have bestowed on us if Adam had not sinned.

title: Was any human person ever preserved from original sin?
body: The Blessed Virgin Mary was preserved from original sin in view of the merits of her Divine Son, and this privilege is called her Immaculate Conception. I will put enmities between you and the woman, between your seed and her seed; he shall crush your

title: Is original sin the only kind of sin?
body: Original sin is not the only kind of sin; there is another kind, called actual sin, which we ourselves commit.

title: What is actual sin?
body: Actual sin is any willful thought, desire, word, action, or omission forbidden by the law of God.

title: How many kinds of actual sin are there?
body: There are two kinds of actual sin: mortal sin and venial sin.

title: What is mortal sin?
body: Mortal sin is a grievous offense against the law of God.

title: Why is this sin called mortal?
body: This sin is called mortal, or deadly, because it deprives the sinner of sanctifying grace, the supernatural life of the soul.

title: Besides depriving the sinner of sanctifying grace, what else does mortal sin do to the soul?
body: Besides depriving the sinner of sanctifying grace, mortal sin makes the soul an enemy of God, takes away the merit of all its good actions, deprives it of the right to everlasting happiness in heaven, and makes it deserving of everlasting punishment in hell.

title: What three things are necessary to make a sin mortal?
body: To make a sin mortal these three things are needed: 1. the thought, desire, word, action, or omission must be seriously wrong or considered seriously wrong; 2. the sinner, must be mindful of the serious wrong; 3. the sinner must fully consent to it.

title: What is venial sin?
body: Venial sin is a less serious offense against the law of God, which does not deprive the soul of sanctifying grace, and which can be pardoned even without sacramental confession.

title: How can a sin be venial?
body: A sin can be venial in two ways: 1. when the evil done is not seriously wrong; 2. when the evil done is seriously wrong, but the sinner sincerely believes it is only slightly wrong, or does not give full consent to it.

title: How does venial sin harm us?
body: Venial sin harms us by making us less fervent in the service of God, by weakening our power to resist mortal sin, and by making us deserving of God's punishments in this life or in purgatory.

title: How can we keep from committing sin?
body: We can keep from committing sin by praying and by receiving the sacraments; by remembering that God is always with us; by recalling that our bodies are temples of the Holy Ghost; by keeping occupied with work or play; by promptly resisting the sources of sin within us; by avoiding the near occasions of sin. And if thy right hand scandalize thee, cut it off and cast it from thee.

title: What are the chief sources of actual sin?
body: The chief sources of actual sin are: pride, covetousness, lust, anger, gluttony, envy, and sloth, and these are commonly called capital sins.

title: Why are these called capital sins?
body: They are called capital sins, not because they, in themselves, are the greatest sins, but because they are the chief reasons why men commit sin.

title: What are the near occasions of sin?
body: The near occasions of sin are all persons, places, or things that may easily lead us into sin.

title: Did God abandon man after Adam fell into sin?
body: God did not abandon man after Adam fell into sin, but promised to send into the world a Saviour to free man from his sins and to reopen to him the gates of heaven.

title: Who is the Saviour of all men?
body: The Saviour of all men is Jesus Christ.

title: What is the chief teaching of the Catholic Church about Jesus Christ?
body: The chief teaching of the Catholic Church about Jesus Christ is that He is God made man.

title: Why is Jesus Christ God?
body: Jesus Christ is God, because He is the only Son of God, having the same Divine nature as His Father. And the high priest said to him, "I adjure thee by the living God that thou tell us whether thou art the Christ, the Son of God."

title: Why is Jesus Christ man?
body: Jesus Christ is man, because He is the son of the Blessed Virgin Mary and has a body and soul like ours.

title: Is Jesus Christ more than one Person?
body: No, Jesus Christ is only one Person; and that Person is the second Person of the Blessed Trinity.

title: How many natures has Jesus Christ?
body: Jesus Christ has two natures: the nature of God and the nature of man.

title: Was the Son of God always man?
body: The Son of God was not always man, but became man at the time of the Incarnation.

title: What is meant by the Incarnation?
body: By the Incarnation is meant that the Son of God, retaining His Divine nature, took to Himself a human nature, that is, a body and soul like ours.

title: How was the Son of God made man?
body: The Son of God was conceived and made man by the power of the Holy Ghost in the womb of the Blessed Virgin Mary.

title: When was the Son of God conceived and made man?
body: The Son of God was conceived and made man on Annunciation Day, the day on which the Angel Gabriel announced to the Blessed Virgin Mary that she was to be the Mother of God.

title: Is Saint Joseph the father of Jesus Christ?
body: Jesus Christ had no human father, but Saint Joseph was the spouse of the Blessed Virgin Mary and the guardian, or foster father, of Christ.

title: When was Christ born?
body: Christ was born of the Blessed Virgin Mary on Christmas Day, in Bethlehem, more than nineteen hundred years ago.

title: How many years did Jesus Christ live on earth?
body: Jesus Christ lived on earth about thirty-three years.

title: How did Jesus Christ spend His life on earth?
body: Jesus Christ spent His childhood, youth and early manhood in the home of His mother Mary and His foster father Joseph, working as a carpenter in the village of Nazareth in Palestine; He spent His last years in the work of His public ministry.

title: What work did Jesus Christ perform in the course of His public ministry?
body: In the course of His public ministry Jesus Christ gave us an example of great virtue, preached the message of salvation, proved the truth of His message through miracles and prophecies, and established the Church with its sacrifice and sacraments for the salvation of men until the end of time.

title: What is meant by the Redemption?
body: By the Redemption is meant that Jesus Christ, as the Redeemer of the whole human race, offered His sufferings and death to God as a fitting sacrifice in satisfaction for the sins of men, and regained for them the right to be children of God and heirs of heaven.

title: What were the chief sufferings of Christ?
body: The chief sufferings of Christ were His bitter agony of soul, His bloody sweat, His cruel scourging, His crowning with thorns, His crucifixion, and His death on the cross. And Jesus, crying with a loud voice, said: "Father into thy hands I commend my spirit."

title: When did Christ die?
body: Christ died on Good Friday.

title: Where did Christ die?
body: Christ died on Golgotha, a place outside the city of Jerusalem.

title: What do we learn from the sufferings and death of Christ?
body: From the sufferings and death of Christ we learn God's love for man and the evil of sin, for which God, who is all-just, demands such great satisfaction.

title: What do we mean when we say in the Apostles' Creed that Christ descended into hell?
body: When we say that Christ descended into hell we mean that, after He died, the soul of Christ descended into a place or state of rest, called limbo, where the souls of the just were waiting for Him.

title: Why did Christ go to limbo?
body: Christ went to limbo to announce to the souls waiting there the joyful news that He had reopened heaven to mankind.

title: Where was Christ's body while His soul was In limbo?
body: While His soul was in limbo, Christ's body was in the holy sepulchre.

title: When did Christ rise from the dead?
body: Christ rose from the dead, glorious and immortal, on Easter Sunday, the third day after His death. And on the first day of the week, very early in the morning, they came to the sepulchre, bringing the spices which they had prepared.

title: Why did Christ rise from the dead?
body: Christ rose from the dead to show that He is true God and to teach us that we, too, shall rise from the dead.

title: Will all men rise from the dead?
body: All men will rise from the dead, but only those who have been faithful to Christ will share in His glory.

title: When did Christ ascend into heaven?
body: Christ ascended, body and soul, into heaven on Ascension Day, forty days after His Resurrection.

title: Why did Christ remain on earth forty days after His Resurrection?
body: Christ remained on earth forty days after His Resurrection to prove that He had truly risen from the dead and to complete the instruction of the apostles.

title: What do we mean when we say that Christ sits at the right hand of God, the Father Almighty?
body: When we say that Christ sits at the right hand of God, the Father Almighty, we mean that Our Lord as God is equal to the Father, and that as man He shares above all the saints in the glory of His Father and exercises for all eternity the supreme authority of a king over all creatures.

title: What do we mean when we say that Christ will come from thence to judge the living and the dead?
body: When we say that Christ will come from thence to judge the living and the dead, we mean that on the last day Our Lord will come to pronounce a sentence of eternal reward or of eternal punishment on everyone who has ever lived in this world. For the Son of Man is to come with his angels in the glory of his Father, and then he will render to

title: Who is the Holy Ghost?
body: The Holy Ghost is God and the third Person of the Blessed Trinity.

title: From whom does the Holy Ghost proceed?
body: The Holy Ghost proceeds from the Father and the Son.

title: Is the Holy Ghost equal to the Father and the Son?
body: The Holy Ghost is equal to the Father and the Son, because He is God.

title: What does the Holy Ghost do for the salvation of mankind?
body: The Holy Ghost dwells in the Church as the source of its life and sanctifies souls through the gift of grace.

title: What is grace?
body: Grace is a supernatural gift of God bestowed on us through the merits of Jesus Christ for our salvation. And of his fullness we have all received, grace for grace.

title: How many kinds of grace are there?
body: There are two kinds of grace: sanctifying grace and actual grace.

title: What is sanctifying grace?
body: Sanctifying grace is that grace which confers on our souls a new life, that is, a sharing in the life of God Himself.

title: What are the chief effects of sanctifying grace?
body: The chief effects of sanctifying grace are: it makes us holy and pleasing to God; it makes us adopted children of God; it makes us temples of the Holy Ghost; it gives us the right to heaven.

title: What is actual grace?
body: Actual grace is a supernatural help of God which enlightens our mind and strengthens our will to do good and to avoid evil.

title: Can we resist the grace of God?
body: We can resist the grace of God, for our will is free, and God does not force us to accept His grace. Jerusalem, Jerusalem!

title: Why is sanctifying grace necessary for salvation?
body: Sanctifying grace is necessary for salvation because it is the supernatural life, which alone enables us to attain the supernatural happiness of heaven.

title: Is actual grace necessary for all who have attained the use of reason?
body: Actual grace is necessary for all who have attained the use of reason, because without it we cannot long resist the power of temptation or perform other actions which merit a reward in heaven.

title: What are the principal ways of obtaining grace?
body: The principal ways of obtaining grace are prayer and the sacraments, especially the Holy Eucharist.

title: How can we make our most ordinary actions merit a heavenly reward?
body: We can make our most ordinary actions merit a heavenly reward by doing them for the love of God and by keeping ourselves in the state of grace.

title: What are the chief supernatural powers that are bestowed on our souls with sanctifying grace?
body: The chief supernatural powers that are bestowed on our souls with sanctifying grace are the three theological virtues and the seven gifts of the Holy Ghost.

title: Why are these virtues called theological virtues?
body: These virtues are called theological virtues because they have God for their proper object.

title: What are the three theological virtues?
body: The three theological virtues are faith, hope, and charity.

title: What is faith?
body: Faith is the virtue by which we firmly believe all the truths God has revealed, on the word of God revealing them, who can neither deceive nor be deceived.

title: What is hope?
body: Hope is the virtue by which we firmly trust that God, who is all-powerful and faithful to His promises, will in His mercy give us eternal happiness and the means to obtain it. But hope that is seen is not hope. For how can a man hope for what he sees?

title: What is charity?
body: Charity is the virtue by which we love God above all things for His own sake, and our neighbor as ourselves for the love of God. If I should speak with the tongues of men and angels, but do not have charity, I have become as a sounding brass or a tinkling cymbal.

title: Which are the seven gifts of the Holy Ghost?
body: The seven gifts of the Holy Ghost are: wisdom, understanding, counsel, fortitude, knowledge, piety, and fear of the Lord. And the spirit of the Lord shall rest upon him; the spirit of wisdom and of understanding, the spirit of counsel and of fortitude, the spirit of knowledge and of godliness.

title: How do the gifts of the Holy Ghost help us?
body: The gifts of the Holy Ghost help us by making us more alert to discern and more ready to do the will of God.

title: Which are some of the effects in us of the gifts of the Holy Ghost?
body: Some of the effects in us of the gifts of the Holy Ghost are the fruits of the Holy Ghost and the beatitudes.

title: Which are the twelve fruits of the Holy Ghost?
body: The twelve fruits of the Holy Ghost are: charity, joy, peace, patience, benignity, goodness, long-suffering, mildness, faith, modesty, continency, and chastity.

title: Which are the eight beatitudes?
body: The eight beatitudes are: Blessed are the poor in spirit, for theirs is the kingdom of heaven. Blessed are the meek, for they shall possess the earth. Blessed are they who mourn, for they shall be comforted. Blessed are they who hunger and thirst for justice, for they shall be satisfied. Blessed are the merciful, for they shall obtain mercy. Blessed are the clean of heart, for they shall see God.

title: Are there any other virtues besides the theological virtues of faith, hope, and charity?
body: Besides the theological virtues of faith, hope, and charity there are other virtues, called moral virtues.

title: Why are these virtues called moral virtues?
body: These virtues are called moral virtues because they dispose us to lead moral, or good lives, by aiding us to treat persons and things in the right way, that is, according to the will of God.

title: Which are the chief moral virtues?
body: The chief moral virtues are: prudence, justice, fortitude, and temperance; these are called cardinal virtues. And if a man love justice, her labors have great virtues.

title: Why are these virtues called cardinal virtues?
body: These virtues are called cardinal virtues because they are like hinges on which hang all the other moral virtues and our whole moral life. The word "cardinal" is derived from the Latin word "cardo" meaning hinge.

title: How do prudence, justice, fortitude and temperance dispose us to lead good lives?
body: Prudence disposes us in all circumstances to form right judgments about what we must do or not do. Justice disposes us to give everyone what belongs to him. Fortitude disposes us to do what is good in spite of any difficulty. Temperance disposes us to control our desires and to use rightly the things which please ourselves.

title: Which are some of the other moral virtues?
body: Some of the other moral virtues are: Filial piety and patriotism, which dispose us to honor, love, and respect our parents and our country. Obedience, which disposes us to do the will of our superiors. Veracity, which disposes us to tell the truth. Liberality, which disposes us rightly to use worldly goods. Patience, which disposes us to bear up under trials and difficulties. Humility, which disposes us to acknowledge our limitations. Chastity, or purity, which disposes us to be pure in soul and body. Besides these, there are many other moral virtues.

title: What is the Church?
body: The Church is the congregation of all baptized persons united in the same true faith, the same sacrifice, and the same sacraments, under the authority of the Sovereign Pontiff and the bishops in communion with him.

title: Who founded the Church?
body: Jesus Christ founded the Church.

title: Why did Jesus Christ found the Church?
body: Jesus Christ founded the Church to bring all men to eternal salvation. My sheep hear my voice, and I know them and they follow me.

title: How is the Church enabled to lead men to salvation?
body: The Church is enabled to lead men to salvation by the indwelling of the Holy Ghost, who gives it life.

title: When was the dwelling of the Holy Ghost in the Church first visibly manifested?
body: The dwelling of the Holy Ghost in the Church was first visibly manifested on Pentecost Sunday, when He came down upon the apostles in the form of tongues of fire. And when the days of Pentecost were drawing to a close, they were all together in one place. And suddenly there came a sound from heaven, as of a violent wind blowing, and it filled the whole house where they were sitting. And there appeared to them parted tongues as of fire, which settled upon each of them.

title: How long will the Holy Ghost dwell in the Church?
body: The Holy Ghost will dwell in the Church until the end of time.

title: Who sent the Holy Ghost to dwell in the Church?
body: God the Father and God the Son sent the Holy Ghost to dwell in the Church.

title: What does the indwelling of the Holy Ghost enable the Church to do?
body: The indwelling of the Holy Ghost enables the Church to teach, to sanctify, and to rule the faithful in the name of Christ. But when he, the Spirit of truth, has come, he will teach you all the truth.

title: What is meant by teaching, sanctifying, and ruling in the name of Christ?
body: By teaching, sanctifying, and ruling in the name of Christ is meant that the Church always does the will of its Divine Founder, who remains forever its invisible Head.

title: To whom did Christ give the power to teach, to sanctify, and to rule the members of His Church?
body: Christ gave the power to teach, to sanctify, and to rule the members of His Church to the apostles, the first bishops of the Church.

title: Did Christ intend that this power should be exercised by the apostles alone?
body: No, Christ intended that this power should be exercised also by their successors, the bishops of the Church.

title: Did Christ give special power in His Church to any one of the apostles?
body: Christ gave special power in His Church to Saint Peter by making him the head of the apostles and the chief teacher and ruler of the entire Church.

title: Did Christ intend that the special power of chief teacher and ruler of the entire Church should be exercised by Saint Peter alone?
body: Christ did not intend that the special power of chief teacher and ruler of the entire Church should be exercised by Saint Peter alone, but intended that this power should be passed down to his successor, the Pope, the Bishop of Rome, who is the Vicar of Christ on earth and the visible head of the Church.

title: Who assist the bishops in the care of souls?
body: The priests, especially parish priests, assist the bishops in the care of souls.

title: Who are the laity of the Church?
body: The laity of the Church are all its members who do not belong to the clerical or to the religious state.

title: How can the laity help the Church in her care of souls?
body: The laity can help the Church in her care of souls by leading lives that will reflect credit on the Church, and by cooperating with their bishops and priests, especially through Catholic Action.

title: What is Catholic Action?
body: Catholic Action is the active participation of the laity in the apostolate of the Church under the guidance of the hierarchy.

title: In what ways can the laity participate actively in the apostolate of the Church?
body: The laity can participate actively in the apostolate of the Church when they arouse the interest of non-Catholics in the Catholic faith; promote high standards in the press, motion pictures, radio and television; take part in the activities of Catholic societies and organizations; represent, under proper direction, the Church's position in speaking and writing; and go as lay missionaries to foreign lands.

title: Which is the one true Church established by Christ?
body: The one true Church established by Christ is the Catholic Church. And other sheep I have that are not of this fold.

title: How do we know that the Catholic Church is the one true Church established by Christ?
body: We know that the Catholic Church is the one true Church established by Christ because it alone has the marks of the true Church.

title: What do we mean by the marks of the Church?
body: By the marks of the Church we mean certain clear signs by which all men can recognize it as the true Church founded by Jesus Christ.

title: What are the chief marks of the Church?
body: The chief marks of the Church are four: It is one, holy, catholic or universal, and apostolic.

title: Why is the Catholic Church one?
body: The Catholic Church is one because all its members, according to the will of Christ, profess the same faith, have the same sacrifice and sacraments, and are united under one and the same visible head, the Pope.

title: Why is the Catholic Church holy?
body: The Catholic Church is holy because it was founded by Jesus Christ, who is all-holy, and because it teaches, according to the will of Christ, holy doctrines, and provides the means of leading a holy life, thereby giving holy members to every age. A good tree cannot bear bad fruit, nor can a bad tree bear good fruit. Every tree that does not bear good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire.

title: Why is the Catholic Church catholic or universal?
body: The Catholic Church is catholic or universal because, destined to last for all time, it never fails to fulfill the divine commandment to teach all nations all the truths revealed by God.

title: Why is the Catholic Church apostolic?
body: The Catholic Church is apostolic because it was founded by Christ on the apostles and, according to His divine will, has always been governed by their lawful successors.

title: How do we know that no other church but the Catholic Church is the true Church of Christ?
body: We know that no other church but the Catholic Church is the true Church of Christ because no other church has these four marks.

title: What are the chief attributes of the Catholic Church?
body: The chief attributes of the Catholic Church are authority, infallibility, and indefectibility. They are called attributes because they are qualities perfecting the nature of the Church.

title: What is meant by the authority of the Catholic Church?
body: By the authority of the Catholic Church is meant that the Pope and the bishops, as the lawful successors of the apostles, have power from Christ Himself to teach, to sanctify, and to govern the faithful in spiritual matters.

title: What is meant by the infallibility of the Catholic Church?
body: By the infallibility of the Catholic Church is meant that the Church, by the special assistance of the Holy Ghost, cannot err when it teaches or believes a doctrine of faith or morals.

title: When does the Church teach infallibly?
body: The Church teaches infallibly when it defines, through the Pope alone, as the teacher of all Christians, or through the Pope and the bishops, a doctrine of faith or morals to be held by all the faithful.

title: What is meant by the indefectibility of the Catholic Church?
body: By the indefectibility of the Catholic Church is meant that the Church, as Christ founded it, will last until the end of time.

title: Are all obliged to belong to the Catholic Church in order to be saved?
body: All are obliged to belong to the Catholic Church in order to be saved. Jesus said to him, "I am the way, the truth, and the life.

title: What do we mean when we say, "Outside the Church there is no salvation?
body: When we say, "Outside the Church there is no salvation," we mean that Christ made the Catholic Church a necessary means of salvation and commanded all to enter it, so that a person must be connected with the Church in some way to be saved.

title: How can persons who are not members of the Catholic Church be saved?
body: Persons who are not members of the Catholic Church can be saved if, through no fault of their own, they do not know that the Catholic Church is the true Church, but they love God and try to do His will, for in this way they are connected with the Church by desire.

title: Why is the Catholic Church called the Mystical Body of Christ?
body: The Catholic Church is called the Mystical Body of Christ because its members are united by supernatural bonds with one another and with Christ, their Head, thus resembling the members and head of the living human body.

title: What conditions are necessary in order that a person be a member of the Mystical Body in the full sense?
body: In order that a person be a member of the Mystical Body in the full sense, it is necessary that he be baptized, that he profess the Catholic faith, and that he neither separate himself from the Mystical Body nor be excluded by lawful authority.

title: How does a baptized person separate himself from full incorporation in the Mystical Body?
body: A baptized person separates himself from full incorporation in the Mystical Body by open and deliberate heresy, apostasy or schism.

title: How does a baptized person separate himself from full incorporation in the Mystical Body by heresy?
body: A baptized person separates himself from full incorporation in the Mystical Body by heresy when he openly rejects or doubts some doctrine proposed by the Catholic Church as a truth of divine-Catholic faith, though still professing himself a Christian.

title: When does a baptized person separate himself from full incorporation in the Mystical Body by apostasy?
body: A baptized person separates himself from full incorporation in the Mystical Body by apostasy when he openly rejects the entire Christian faith.

title: When does a baptized person separate himself from full incorporation in the Mystical Body by schism?
body: A baptized person separates himself from full incorporation in the Mystical Body by schism when he openly refuses obedience to the lawful authorities of the Church, particularly to the Pope.

title: When is a baptized person separated from full incorporation in the Mystical Body by lawful authority?
body: A baptized person is separated from full incorporation in the Mystical Body by lawful authority when he incurs one of the more severe forms of excommunication.

title: What is meant by "the communion of saints" in the Apostles' Creed?
body: By "the communion of saints" is meant the union of the faithful on earth, the blessed in heaven, and the souls in purgatory, with Christ as their Head.

title: Through the communion of saints, what can the blessed in heaven do for the souls in purgatory and the faithful on earth?
body: Through the communion of saints, the blessed in heaven can help the souls in purgatory and the faithful on earth by praying for them.

title: Should the faithful on earth, through the communion of saints, honor the blessed in heaven and pray to them?
body: The faithful on earth, through the communion of saints, should honor the blessed in heaven and pray to them, because they are worthy of honor and as friends of God will help the faithful on earth.

title: Can the faithful on earth, through the communion of saints, relieve the sufferings of the souls in purgatory?
body: The faithful on earth, through the communion of saints, can relieve the sufferings the souls in purgatory by prayer, fasting, and other good works, by indulgences, and by having Masses offered for them.

title: Can the faithful on earth help one another?
body: The faithful on earth, as members of the Mystical Body of Christ, can help one another by practicing supernatural charity and the spiritual and corporal works of mercy. Pray for one another that you may be saved.

title: What is meant in the Apostles' Creed by "the forgiveness of sins"?
body: By "the forgiveness of sins" in the Apostles' Creed is meant that God has given to the Church, through Jesus Christ, the power to forgive sins, no matter how great or how many they are, if sinners truly repent. Receive the Holy Ghost; whose sins you shall forgive, they are forgiven them; and whose sins you shall

title: What is meant by "the resurrection of the body"?
body: By "the resurrection of the body" is meant that at the end of the world the bodies of all men will rise from the earth and be united again to their souls, nevermore to be separated. Behold, I tell you a mystery.

title: Why will the bodies of the just rise?
body: The bodies of the just will rise to share forever in the glory of their souls.

title: Has the body of any human person ever been raised from the dead and taken into heaven?
body: By the special privilege of her Assumption, the body of the Blessed Virgin Mary, united to her immaculate soul, was glorified and taken into heaven.

title: Why will the bodies of the damned also rise?
body: The bodies of the damned will also rise to share in the eternal punishment of their souls.

title: What is the judgment called which will be passed on all men immediately after the general resurrection?
body: The judgment which will be passed on all men immediately after the general resurrection is called the general judgment.

title: What is the judgment called which will be passed on each one of us immediately after death?
body: The judgment which will be passed on each one of us immediately after death is called the particular judgment.

title: If everyone is judged immediately after death, why will there be a general judgment?
body: Although everyone is judged immediately after death, it is fitting that there be a general judgment in order that the justice, wisdom, and mercy of God may be glorified in the presence of all. Then shall the just stand with great constancy against those that have afflicted them and taken away their labors.

title: What are the rewards or punishments appointed for men after the particular judgment?
body: The rewards or punishments appointed for men after the particular judgment are heaven, purgatory, or hell.

title: Who are punished in purgatory?
body: Those are punished for a time in purgatory who die in the state of grace but are guilty of venial sin, or have not fully satisfied for the temporal punishment due to their sins.

title: Who are punished in hell?
body: Those are punished in hell who die in mortal sin; they are deprived of the vision of God and suffer dreadful torments, especially that of fire, for all eternity.

title: Who are rewarded in heaven?
body: Those are rewarded in heaven who have died in the state of grace and have been purified in purgatory, if necessary, from all venial sin and all debt of temporal punishment; they see God face to face and share forever in His glory and happiness.

title: What is meant by the word "Amen," with which we end the Apostles' Creed?
body: By the word "Amen," with which we end the Apostles' Creed, is meant "So it is," or "So be it"; the word expresses our firm belief in all the doctrines that the Creed contains.

title: Besides believing what God has revealed, what else must we do to be saved?
body: Besides believing what God has revealed, we must keep His law.

title: Which are the two great commandments that contain the whole law of God?
body: The two great commandments that contain the whole law of God are: 1. Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with thy whole heart, and with thy whole soul, and with thy whole mind, and with thy whole strength; 2. Thou shalt love thy neighbor as thyself. And one of them, a doctor of the Law, putting him to the test, asked him, "Master, which is the great commandment in the Law?" Jesus said to him, "'Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with thy whole heart, and with thy whole soul, and with thy whole mind.' This is the greatest and the first commandment.

title: What must we do to love God, our neighbor, and ourselves?
body: To love God, our neighbor, and ourselves we must keep the commandments of God and of the Church, and perform the spiritual and corporal works of mercy.

title: Which are the chief corporal works of mercy?
body: The chief corporal works of mercy are seven: To feed the hungry. To give drink to the thirsty. To clothe the naked. To visit the imprisoned. To shelter the homeless. To visit the sick. To bury the dead.

title: Which are the chief spiritual works of mercy?
body: The chief spiritual works of mercy are seven: To admonish the sinner. To instruct the ignorant. To counsel the doubtful. To comfort the sorrowful. To bear wrongs patiently. To forgive all injuries. To pray for the living and the dead.

title: Is everyone obliged to perform the works of mercy?
body: Everyone is obliged to perform the works of mercy, according to his own ability and the need of his neighbor.

title: Are all the ordinary deeds done every day to relieve the corporal or spiritual needs of others true works of mercy?
body: All the ordinary deeds done every day to relieve the corporal or spiritual needs of others are true works of mercy, if done in the name of Christ.

title: Which are the commandments of God?
body: The commandments of God are these ten: 1. I am the Lord thy God; thou shalt not have strange gods before Me. 2. Thou shalt not take the name of the Lord thy God in vain. 3. Remember thou keep holy the Lord's day. 4. Honor thy father and thy mother. 5. Thou shalt not kill. 6. Thou shalt not commit adultery. 7. Thou shalt not steal. 8. Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy neighbor. 9. Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's wife. 10. Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's goods.

title: Should we be satisfied merely to keep the commandments God?
body: We should not be satisfied merely to keep the commandments of God, but should always be ready to do good deeds, even when they are not commanded.

title: What does Our Savior especially recommend that is not strictly commanded by the law of God?
body: Our Savior especially recommends the observance of the Evangelical Counsels – voluntary poverty, perpetual chastity, and perfect obedience.

title: What is the first commandment of God?
body: The first commandment of God is: I am the Lord thy God; thou shalt not have strange gods before Me. Thou shalt not have strange Gods before me.

title: What are we commanded by the first commandment?
body: By the first commandment we are commanded to offer to God alone the supreme worship that is due Him.

title: How do we worship God?
body: We worship God by acts of faith, hope, and charity, and by adoring Him and praying to Him.

title: What does faith oblige us to do?
body: Faith obliges us: first, to make efforts to find out what God has revealed; second, to believe firmly what God has revealed; third, to profess our faith openly whenever necessary.

title: What does hope oblige us to do?
body: Hope obliges us to trust firmly that God will give us eternal life and the means to obtain it.

title: What does charity oblige us to do?
body: Charity obliges us to love God above all things because He is infinitely good, and to love our neighbor as ourselves for the love of God. And one of them, a doctor of the Law, putting him to the test, asked him, "Master, which is the great commandment in the Law?" Jesus said to him, "'Thou shalt love the Lord thy God with thy whole heart, and with thy whole soul, and with thy whole mind.' This is the greatest and the first commandment.

title: How can a Catholic best safeguard his faith?
body: A Catholic can best safeguard his faith by making frequent acts of faith, by praying for a strong faith, by studying his religion very earnestly, by living a good life, by good reading, by refusing to associate with the enemies of the Church, and by not reading books and papers opposed to the Church and her teaching. I know that after my departure fierce wolves will get in among you, and will not spare the flock.

title: How does a Catholic sin against faith?
body: A Catholic sins against faith by apostasy, heresy, indifferentism, and by taking part in non-Catholic worship.

title: Why does a Catholic sin against faith by taking part in non-Catholic worship.
body: A Catholic sins against faith by taking part in non-Catholic worship when he intends to identify himself with a religion he knows is defective. This is why I was born, and why I have come into the world, to bear witness to the truth.

title: What are the sins against hope?
body: The sins against hope are presumption and despair.

title: When does a person sin by presumption?
body: A person sins by presumption when he trusts that he can be saved by his own efforts without God's help, or by God's help without his own efforts. Nay I do not even judge my own self.

title: When does a person sin by despair?
body: A person sins by despair when he deliberately refuses to trust that God will give him the necessary help to save his soul. May no temptation take hold of you but such as man is equal to.

title: What are the chief sins against charity?
body: The chief sins against charity are hatred of God and of our neighbor, envy, sloth, and scandal.

title: Besides the sins against faith, hope, and charity, what other sins does the first commandment forbid?
body: Besides the sins against faith, hope, and charity, the first commandment forbids also superstition and sacrilege.

title: When does a person sin by superstition?
body: A person sins by superstition when he attributes to a creature a power that belongs to God alone, as when he makes use of charms or spells, believes in dreams or fortune-telling, or goes to spiritists. Neither let there be found among you any one that ... consulteth soothsayers, or observeth dreams and omens.

title: When does a person sin by sacrilege?
body: A person sins by sacrilege when he mistreats sacred persons, places, or things. They have set thy sanctuary ablaze, they have profaned the dwelling of thy name on the earth. (Psalm

title: Does the first commandment forbid us to honor the saints in heaven?
body: The first commandment does not forbid us to honor the saints in heaven, provided we do not give them the honor that belongs to God alone.

title: Why do we honor the saints in heaven?
body: We honor the saints in heaven because they practiced great virtue when they were on earth, and because in honoring those who are the chosen friends of God we honor God Himself.

title: How can we honor the saints?
body: We can honor the saints: first, by imitating their holy lives; second, by praying to them; third, by showing respect to their relics and images.

title: When we pray to the saints what do we ask them to do?
body: When we pray to the saints we ask them to offer their prayers to God for us.

title: How do we know that the saints will pray for us?
body: We know that the saints will pray for us because they are with God and have great love for us.

title: Why do we honor relics?
body: We honor relics because they are the bodies of the saints or objects connected with the saints or with Our Lord.

title: When does the first commandment forbid the making or the use of statues and pictures?
body: The first commandment forbids the making or the use of statues and pictures only when they promote false worship. Thou shalt not have strange Gods before me.

title: Is it right to show respect to the statues and pictures of Christ and of the saints?
body: It is right to show respect to the statues and pictures of Christ and of the saints, just as it is right to show respect to the images of those whom we honor or love on earth.

title: Do we honor Christ and the saints when we pray before the crucifix, relics, and sacred images?
body: We honor Christ and the saints when we pray before the crucifix, relics, and sacred images because we honor the persons they represent; we adore Christ and venerate the saints.

title: Do we pray to the crucifix or to the images and relics of the saints?
body: We do not pray to the crucifix or to the images and relics of the saints, but to the persons they represent.

title: What is the second commandment of God?
body: The second commandment of God is: Thou shalt not take the name of the Lord thy God in vain.

title: What are we commanded by the second commandment?
body: By the second commandment we are commanded always to speak with reverence of God, of the saints, and of holy things, and to be truthful in taking oaths and faithful to them and to our vows. Blessed be the name of the Lord both now and forever.

title: What is an oath?
body: An oath is the calling on God to witness the truth of what we say.

title: What things are necessary to make an oath lawful?
body: To make an oath lawful, three things are necessary: first, we must have a good reason for taking an oath; second, we must be convinced that what we say under oath is true; third, we must not swear, that is, take an oath, to do what is wrong.

title: What great sin does a person commit who deliberately calls on God to bear witness to a lie?
body: A person who deliberately calls on God to bear witness to a lie commits the very grievous sin of perjury.

title: What is a vow?
body: A vow is a deliberate promise made to God by which a person binds himself under pain of sin to do something that is especially pleasing to God.

title: What is meant by taking God's name in vain?
body: By taking God's name in vain is meant that the name of God or the holy name of Jesus Christ is used without reverence; for example, to express surprise or anger. Blessed be the name of the Lord both now and forever.

title: Is it a sin to take God's name in vain?
body: It is a sin to take God's name in vain; ordinarily, it is a venial sin.

title: What is cursing?
body: Cursing is the calling down of some evil on a person, place, or thing.

title: What is blasphemy?
body: Blasphemy is insulting language which expresses contempt for God, either directly or through His saints and holy things.

title: What is the third commandment of God?
body: The third commandment of God is: Remember thou keep holy the Lord's day.

title: Why does the Church command us to keep Sunday as the Lord's day?
body: The Church commands us to keep Sunday as the Lord's day, because on Sunday Christ rose from the dead, and on Sunday the Holy Ghost descended upon the apostles.

title: What are we commanded by the third commandment?
body: By the third commandment we are commanded to worship God in a special manner on Sunday, the Lord's day.

title: How does the Church command us to worship God on Sunday?
body: The Church commands us to worship God on Sunday by assisting at the Holy Sacrifice of the Mass.

title: What is forbidden by the third commandment of God?
body: By the third commandment of God all unnecessary servile work on Sunday is forbidden.

title: What is servile work?
body: Servile work is that which requires labor of body rather than of mind.

title: When is servile work allowed on Sunday?
body: Servile work is allowed on Sunday when the honor of God, our own need, or that of our neighbor requires it.

title: What is the fourth commandment of God?
body: The fourth commandment of God is: Honor thy father and thy mother.

title: What are we commanded by the fourth commandment?
body: By the fourth commandment we are commanded to respect and love our parents, to obey them in all that is not sinful, and to help them when they are in need.

title: Does the fourth commandment oblige us to respect and to obey others besides our parents?
body: Besides our parents, the fourth commandment obliges us to respect and obey all our lawful superiors. Let everyone be subject to the higher authorities, for there exists no authority except from God, and those who exist have been appointed by God.

title: What duty have parents toward their children and superiors toward those under their care?
body: Parents must provide for the spiritual and bodily welfare of their children; superiors, according to their varying degrees of responsibility, must care for those entrusted to them.

title: What are the duties of a citizen toward his country?
body: A citizen must love his country, be sincerely interested in its welfare, and respect and obey its lawful authority.

title: How does a citizen show a sincere interest in his country's welfare?
body: A citizen shows a sincere interest in his country's welfare by voting honestly and without selfish motives, by paying just taxes, and by defending his country's rights when necessary.

title: Why must we respect and obey the lawful authority of our country?
body: We must respect and obey the lawful authority of our country because it comes from God, the Source of all authority.

title: Why are we obliged to take an active part in works of good citizenship?
body: We are obliged to take an active part in works of good citizenship because right reason requires citizens to work together for the public welfare of the country.

title: What are the chief duties of those who hold public office?
body: The chief duties of those who hold public office are to be just to all in exercising their authority and to promote the general welfare.

title: What does the fourth commandment forbid?
body: The fourth commandment forbids disrespect, unkindness, and disobedience to our parents and lawful superiors.

title: What is the fifth commandment of God?
body: The fifth commandment of God is: Thou shalt not kill.

title: What are we commanded by the fifth commandment?
body: By the fifth commandment we are commanded to take proper care of our own spiritual and bodily well-being and that of our neighbor.

title: What does the fifth commandment forbid?
body: The fifth commandment forbids murder and suicide, and also fighting, anger, hatred, revenge, drunkenness, reckless driving, and bad example. Everyone who hates his brother is a murderer.

title: What is the sixth commandment of God?
body: The sixth commandment of God is: Thou shalt not commit adultery.

title: What are we commanded by the sixth commandment?
body: By the sixth commandment we are commanded to be pure and modest in our behavior.

title: What does the sixth commandment forbid?
body: The sixth commandment forbids all impurity and immodesty in words, looks, and actions, whether alone or with others.

title: What are the chief dangers to the virtue of chastity?
body: The chief dangers to the virtue of chastity are: idleness, sinful curiosity, bad companions, drinking, immodest dress, and indecent books, plays, and motion pictures.

title: What are the chief means of preserving the virtue of chastity?
body: The chief means of preserving the virtue of chastity are to avoid carefully all unnecessary dangers, to seek God's help through prayer, frequent confession, Holy Communion, and assistance at Holy Mass, and to have a special devotion to the Blessed Virgin. Be sober, be watchful! For your adversary, the devil, as a roaring lion, goes about seeking someone to

title: What is the seventh commandment of God?
body: The seventh commandment of God is: Thou shalt not steal.

title: What are we commanded by the seventh commandment?
body: By the seventh commandment we are commanded to respect what belongs to others, to live up to our business agreements, and to pay our just debts.

title: What does the seventh commandment forbid?
body: Besides stealing, the seventh commandment forbids cheating, unjust keeping of what belongs to others, unjust damage to the property of others, and the accepting of bribes by public officials. Do not any unjust thing in judgment, in rule, in weight, or in measure.

title: Are we obliged to restore to the owner stolen goods, or their value?
body: We are obliged to restore to the owner stolen goods, or their value, whenever we are able.

title: Are we obliged to repair damage unjustly done to the property of others?
body: We are obliged to repair damage unjustly done to the property of others, or to pay the amount of the damage, as far as we are able.

title: What is the eighth commandment of God?
body: The eighth commandment of God is: Thou shalt not bear false witness against thy neighbor.

title: What are we commanded by the eighth commandment?
body: By the eighth commandment we are commanded to speak the truth in all things, but especially in what concerns the good name and honor of others.

title: What does the eighth commandment forbid?
body: The eighth commandment forbids lies, rash judgment, detraction, calumny, and the telling of secrets we are bound to keep.

title: When does a person commit the sin of rash judgment?
body: A person commits the sin of rash judgment when, without sufficient reason, he believes something harmful to another's character.

title: When does a person commit the sin of detraction?
body: A person commits the sin of detraction when, without a good reason, he makes known the hidden faults of another.

title: When does a person commit the sin of calumny or slander?
body: A person commits the sin of calumny or slander when by lying he injures the good name of another.

title: When are we obliged to keep a secret?
body: We are obliged to keep a secret when we have promised to do so, when our office requires it, or when the good of another demands it.

title: What must a person do who has sinned by detraction or calumny, or has told a secret he is bound to keep?
body: A person who has sinned by detraction or calumny, or who has told a secret he is bound to keep, must repair the harm he has done to his neighbor, as far as he is able.

title: What is the ninth commandment of God?
body: The ninth commandment of God is: Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's wife.

title: What are we commanded by the ninth commandment?
body: By the ninth commandment we are commanded to be pure in thought and desire.

title: Are mere thoughts about impure things always sinful in themselves?
body: Mere thoughts about impure things are not always sinful in themselves, but such thoughts are dangerous.

title: When do thoughts about impure things become sinful?
body: Thoughts about impure things become sinful when a person thinks of an unchaste act and deliberately takes pleasure in so thinking, or when unchaste desire or passion is aroused and consent is given to it.

title: What is forbidden by the ninth commandment?
body: The ninth commandment forbids all thoughts and desires contrary to chastity.

title: What is the tenth commandment of God?
body: The tenth commandment of God is: Thou shalt not covet thy neighbor's goods.

title: What does the tenth commandment forbid?
body: The tenth commandment forbids all desire to take or to keep unjustly what belongs to others, and also forbids envy at their success. Take heed and guard yourself from all covetousness, for a man's life does not consist in the abundance of

title: Whence has the Catholic Church the right to make laws?
body: The Catholic Church has the right to make laws from Jesus Christ, who said to the apostles, the first bishops of His Church: "Whatever you bind on earth shall be bound also in heaven."

title: By whom is this right to make laws exercised?
body: This right to make laws is exercised by the bishops, the successors of the apostles, and especially by the Pope, who as the successor of the chief of the apostles, Saint Peter, has the right to make laws for the Universal Church.

title: Which are the chief commandments, or laws, of the Church?
body: The chief commandments, or laws, of the Church are these six: To assist at Mass on all Sundays and holydays of obligation. To fast and to abstain on the days appointed. To confess our sins at least once a year. To receive Holy Communion during the Easter time. To contribute to the support of the Church. To observe the laws of the Church concerning marriage.

title: What sin does a Catholic commit who through his own fault misses Mass on a Sunday or holyday of obligation?
body: A Catholic who through his own fault misses Mass on a Sunday or holyday of obligation commits a mortal sin.

title: What else does the Church oblige us to do on holy days of obligation?
body: The Church obliges us to abstain from servile work on holy days of obligation, just as on Sundays, as far as we are able.

title: Why were holy days instituted by the Church?
body: Holy days were instituted by the Church to remind us of the mysteries of our religion and of the important events in the lives of Christ and of His Blessed Mother, and to recall to us the virtues and the rewards of the saints.

title: What is a fast day?
body: A fast day is a day on which only one full meal is allowed, but in the morning and evening some food may be taken, the quantity and quality of which are determined by approved local custom.

title: Who are obliged to observe the fast days of the Church?
body: All baptized persons between the ages of twenty-one and fifty-nine are obliged to observe the fast days of the Church, unless they are excused or dispensed.

title: What is a day of abstinence?
body: A day of abstinence is a day on which we are not allowed the use of meat.

title: Who are obliged to observe the abstinence days of the Church?
body: All Catholics who have passed their fourteenth birthday and have attained the use of reason are obliged to observe the abstinence days of the Church, unless excused or dispensed.

title: Why does the Church command us to fast and to abstain?
body: The Church commands us to fast and to abstain in order that we may control the desires of the flesh, raise our minds more freely to God, and make satisfaction for sin. But thou, when thou dost fast, anoint thy head wash thy face, so that thou mayest not be seen fasting by men, but by thy Father, who is in secret; and thy Father, who sees in secret, will reward thee. (Matthew 6:18) 291.Why does the Church make Fridays of Lent days of abstinence? The Church makes Fridays of Lent days of abstinence in order that we may do penance for our sins, and also in order that we may prepare ourselves more worthily for Good Friday, when we commemorate the death of Jesus Christ.

title: How can we know the days appointed for fast or abstinence?
body: We can know the days appointed for fast or abstinence from the instructions of our bishops and priests.

title: What is meant by the commandment to confess our sins at least once a year?
body: By the commandment to confess our sins at least once a year is meant that we are strictly obliged to make a good confession within the year, if we have a mortal sin to confess.

title: Why should we go to confession frequently?
body: We should go to confession frequently because frequent confession greatly helps us to overcome temptation, to keep in the state of grace, and to grow in virtue.

title: What sin does a Catholic commit who neglects to receive Holy Communion worthily during the Easter time?
body: A Catholic who neglects to receive Holy Communion worthily during the Easter time commits a mortal sin. He who eats my flesh and drinks my blood has life everlasting and I will raise him up on the last day.

title: What is the Easter time in the United States?
body: The Easter time in the United States begins on the first Sunday of Lent and ends on Trinity Sunday.

title: What is meant by the commandment to contribute to the support of the Church?
body: By the commandment to contribute to the support of the Church is meant that each of us is obliged to bear his fair share of the financial burden of the Holy See, of the diocese, and of the parish.

title: What is the ordinary law of the Church to be observed at the wedding of a Catholic?
body: The ordinary law of the Church to be observed at the wedding of a Catholic is this: A Catholic can contract a true marriage only in the presence of an authorized priest and two witnesses.

title: Does the Church forbid Catholics to contract marriage with certain persons?
body: The Church does forbid Catholics to contract marriage with certain persons, and the following are examples: first, a marriage with a non-Catholic; this is a mixed marriage; second, a marriage with a second cousin, or any relative closer than a second cousin.

title: Why does the Church forbid Catholics to marry non-Catholics?
body: The Church forbids Catholics to marry non-Catholics because mixed marriages often bring about family discord, loss of faith on the part of the Catholic, and neglect of the religious training of the children.

title: Does the Church ever permit mixed marriages or marriages between close relatives?
body: For grave reasons the Church sometimes permits mixed marriages or marriages between close relatives; such a permission is called a dispensation.

title: Does the Church allow Catholics to marry during Lent and Advent?
body: The Church allows Catholics to marry during Lent and Advent, though they should do so without much festivity. A Nuptial Mass is now allowed during these seasons.

title: What is a Nuptial Mass?
body: A Nuptial Mass is a Mass which has special prayers to beg God's blessing on the married couple.

title: What is a sacrament?
body: A sacrament is an outward sign instituted by Christ to give grace.

title: How many sacraments are there?
body: There are seven sacraments: Baptism, Confirmation, Holy Eucharist, Penance, Anointing of the Sick, Holy Orders, and Matrimony.

title: From whom do the sacraments receive their power to give grace?
body: The sacraments receive their power to give grace from God, through the merits of Jesus Christ.

title: Do the sacraments give sanctifying grace?
body: The sacraments do give sanctifying grace.

title: Does each of the sacraments also give a special grace?
body: Each of the sacraments also gives a special grace, called sacramental grace, which helps one to carry out the particular purpose of that sacrament.

title: Do the sacraments always give grace?
body: The sacraments always give grace if we receive them with the right dispositions.

title: Why are Baptism and Penance called sacraments of the dead?
body: Baptism and Penance are called sacraments of the dead because their chief purpose is to give the supernatural life of sanctifying grace to souls spiritually dead through sin.

title: Why are Confirmation, Holy Eucharist, Anointing of the Sick, Holy Orders, and Matrimony called sacraments of the living?
body: Confirmation, Holy Eucharist, Anointing of the Sick, Holy Orders, and Matrimony are called sacraments of the living because their chief purpose is to give more grace to souls already spiritually alive through sanctifying grace.

title: What sin does one commit who knowingly receives a sacrament of the living in mortal sin?
body: He who knowingly receives a sacrament of the living in mortal sin commits a mortal sin of sacrilege, because he treats a sacred thing with grave irreverence.

title: Which are the sacraments that can be received only once?
body: The sacraments that can be received only once are Baptism, Confirmation, and Holy Orders.

title: Why can Baptism, Confirmation, and Holy Orders be received only once?
body: Baptism, Confirmation, and Holy Orders can be received only once because they imprint on the soul a spiritual mark, called a character, which lasts forever. And do not grieve the Holy Ghost of God, in whom you were sealed for the day of redemption. (Ephesians

title: What is Baptism?
body: Baptism is the sacrament that gives our souls the new life of sanctifying grace by which we become children of God and heirs of heaven.

title: What sins does Baptism take away?
body: Baptism takes away original sin; and also actual sin and all the punishment due to them, if the person baptized be guilty of any actual sins and truly sorry for them.

title: What are the effects of the character imprinted on the soul by Baptism?
body: The effects of the character imprinted on the soul by Baptism are that we become members of the Church, subject to its laws, and capable of receiving other sacraments.

title: Who can administer Baptism?
body: The priest is the usual minister of Baptism, but if there is danger that someone will die without Baptism, anyone else may and should baptize.

title: How would you give Baptism?
body: I would give Baptism by pouring ordinary water on the forehead of the person to be baptized, saying while pouring it: "I baptize thee in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost."

title: Why is Baptism necessary for the salvation of all men?
body: Baptism is necessary for the salvation of all men because Christ has said: "Unless a man be born again of water and the spirit, he cannot enter into the kingdom of God."

title: How can those be saved who through no fault of their own have not received the sacrament of Baptism?
body: Those who through no fault of their own have not received the sacrament of Baptism can be saved through what is called baptism of blood or baptism of desire.

title: How does an unbaptized person receive the baptism of blood?
body: An unbaptized person receives the baptism of blood when he suffers martyrdom for the faith of Christ.

title: How does an unbaptized person receive the baptism of desire?
body: An unbaptized person receives the baptism of desire when he loves God above all things and desires to do all that is necessary for his salvation.

title: When should children be baptized?
body: Children should be baptized as soon as possible after birth.

title: What sin do Catholic parents commit who put off for a long time, or entirely neglect, the Baptism of their children?
body: Catholic parents who put off for a long time, or entirely neglect, the Baptism of their children, commit a mortal sin.

title: What do we promise through our godparents in Baptism?
body: We promise through our godparents in Baptism to renounce the devil and to live according to the teachings of Christ and of His Church.

title: Why is the name of a saint given in Baptism?
body: The name of a saint is given in Baptism in order that the person baptized may imitate his virtues and have him for a protector.

title: What is the duty of a godparent after Baptism?
body: The duty of a godparent after Baptism is to see that the child is brought up a good Catholic, if this is not done by the parents.

title: Who should be chosen as godparents for Baptism?
body: Only Catholics who know their faith and live up to the duties of their religion should be chosen as godparents for Baptism.

title: What is Confirmation?
body: Confirmation is the sacrament through which the Holy Ghost comes to us in a special way and enables us to profess our faith as strong and perfect Christians and soldiers of Jesus Christ. But I speak the truth to you; it is expedient for you that I depart.

title: Who is the usual minister of Confirmation?
body: The bishop is the usual minister of Confirmation.

title: What does the bishop do when he gives Confirmation?
body: The bishop extends his hands over those who are to be confirmed, prays that they may receive the Holy Ghost, and, while laying his hand on the head of each person, anoints the forehead with holy chrism in the form of a cross.

title: What does the bishop say in anointing the person he confirms?
body: In anointing the person he confirms, the bishop says: "I sign you with the sign of the cross and I confirm you with the chrism of salvation, in the name of the Father, and of the Son, and of the Holy Ghost."

title: What is holy chrism?
body: Holy chrism is a mixture of olive oil and balm, blessed by the bishop on Holy Thursday.

title: What does the anointing of the forehead with chrism in the form of a cross signify?
body: The anointing of the forehead with chrism in the form of a cross signifies that the Catholic who is confirmed must always be ready to profess his faith openly and to practice it fearlessly.

title: Why does the bishop give the person he confirms a slight blow on the cheek?
body: The bishop gives the person he confirms a slight blow on the cheek to remind him that he must be ready to suffer everything, even death, for the sake of Christ.

title: What are the effects of Confirmation?
body: Confirmation increases sanctifying grace, gives its special sacramental grace, and imprints a lasting character on the soul.
